## Human emerging respiratory pathogens bulletin: Issue 38, February 2020

Organization: Public Health Agency of Canada
Date published: 2020-03-10
Monthly situational analysis of emerging respiratory  diseases affecting humans (data to February 29, 2020)
- Novel coronavirus update
- Avian influenza updates
- Swine influenza updates
- Middle East respiratory syndrome coronavirus (MERS-CoV) update

## Novel coronavirus update
On December 31, 2019, cases of a pneumonia of unknown  etiology were reported in Wuhan, China which has since been determined to be due  to a novel coronavirus (2019-nCoV). On January 30, 2020, the World Health  Organization (WHO) declared the outbreak a Public Health Emergency of  International Concern.
Twenty-four cases of COVID-2019 have  been reported in Canada as of February 29, 2020. This includes 15 cases in  Ontario, eight in British Columbia and one in Quebec. Over 80,000 thousand  cases have been reported globally, involving each continent, with 90% occurring  in China.
The Public Health Agency of Canada is  monitoring the situation closely .

## Avian influenza updates

### Avian influenza A(H7N9)
No new H7N9 cases were reported to the WHO in February 2020.The last case was reported in April  2019. Two travel-related cases were reported in Canada in January 2015. A total  of 1568 human cases of avian influenza A(H7N9), including at least 615 deaths,  have been reported globally since 2013 (Figure 1).
Note: Graph was prepared by the Centre  for Immunization and Respiratory Infectious Diseases (CIRID) using data from  the latest WHO Monthly Influenza at the Human-Animal Interface Risk Assessment.  This graph reflects data available through these risk assessments as of February  29, 2020.
The temporal distribution of avian influenza A(H7N9), globally, January 1st, 2013 – February 29, 2020, has been displayed in Figure 1. The highest number of reports occurred in 2017, with a peak in January. Three cases were reported in 2018 and one case was reported in 2019.

### Avian influenza A(H9N2)
One new case of H9N2 was reported to the WHO in February 2020.  The case was a 7-year-old boy from China who  was diagnosed in Hong Kong after visiting a private home in Shenzhen where  poultry were kept. Globally, 55 cases, including one death, have been reported  since 1998.

### Avian influenza A(H5N1)
No new H5N1 cases were reported to the WHO in February 2020.  The most recent case of H5N1 was reported in March 25, 2019 in Nepal. A total  of 879 cases including 461 deaths have been reported globally since 1997. One  fatal travel-related case of H5N1 was reported in Canada in January 2014.

### Avian influenza A(H5N6)
No new cases of H5N6 were reported to the WHO in February 2020.  The most recent case of H5N6 was reported in China in August 2019. There have  been a total of 24 cases, including 7 deaths, reported globally since 2014, all  in China.

### Avian influenza A(H7N4)
No new cases of  H7N4 were reported to the WHO in February 2020. On February 14, 2018, the WHO  was notified of the first known human case of H7N4. The case was associated  with poultry exposure. No cases have been reported since.

## Swine influenza updates

### Swine origin influenza A(H3N2)v
The most recent case of swine origin influenza H3N2v was  reported in June 2018 in the United States. The case reported exposure to swine  at an agricultural fair. A total of 435 cases, including 1 death, have been  reported globally since 2011. One locally-acquired case of H3N2v was reported  in Canada in December 2016.

### Swine origin influenza A(H1N2)v
The most recent case of swine origin influenza H1N2v was  reported in August 2018 in the United States. The case reported exposure to swine  at an agricultural fair. A total of 26 confirmed cases of H1N2v have been  reported in the United States since 2005. Most of the reported cases resulted  in mild illness.

### Swine origin influenza A(H1N1)v
The most recent case of swine origin influenza H1N1v was  reported in May 2019 in the United States. The case was over 65 years old, had  no history of swine exposure, and has fully recovered. A total of 23 cases have  been reported globally since 2005. To date, the reported H1N1v infections have  been associated with mild illness.
Note: Map  was prepared by the Centre for Immunization and Respiratory Infectious Diseases  (CIRID) using data from the latest WHO Monthly Influenza at the Human-Animal  Interface Risk Assessment. This map  reflects data available through these risk assessments as of February 29, 2020.
The spatial distribution of avian and swine influenza human cases in February 2020 has been displayed in Figure 2. One case of  avian influenza A(H9N2) has been reported in February 2020.

## Middle East respiratory syndrome  coronavirus (MERS-CoV) update
In  February 2020, 19 cases of MERS-CoV were reported.  Eighteen cases,  including two deaths, were reported across nine regions of Saudi Arabia.  Additionally, one case in Qatar was reported (Figure 3, Figure 4). Most of the  cases were male (n=18, 95%), and the median age was 58 years (range: 28 to 81  years). Eighteen cases (95%) had known primary exposure, meaning it is likely  that they acquired the infection from a dromedary camel. No cases were  healthcare workers. A total of 2557 laboratory-confirmed cases of MERS-CoV,  including 866 deaths, have been reported globally since 2012 by the WHO. No  cases have been reported in Canada.
Note : Map was  prepared by the Centre for Immunization and Respiratory Infectious Diseases  (CIRID) using data from the latest WHO Disease Outbreak News and Saudi Arabia’s  Ministry of Health. This map reflects data available through these risk assessments  as of February 29, 2020.
The spatial distribution of reported MERS cases in February 2020 has been displayed in Figure 3. A total of 19 cases have been reported in February.
Note : Graph was  prepared by the Centre for Immunization and Respiratory Infectious Diseases  (CIRID) using data from the WHO Disease Outbreak News and Saudi Arabia’s  Ministry of Health. This graph reflects data available as of February 29, 2020.
The temporal distribution of MERS-CoV, globally, January 1st, 2018– February 29th, 2020, has been displayed in Figure 4.
